nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; reef flats, lagoons, and seaward reefs over coral, rock, pavement, and rubble substrates. FishBase records a depth range of 0–60 m, typically 6–30 m. Notable as the widest-ranging Ctenochaetus, the only member of the genus extending into the Red Sea.

decorPreferences: abundant live rock with biofilm and algal turf for grazing
long horizontal footprint preferred over tall depth
rockwork crevices and overhangs for shelter
moderate to strong water flow (10–20x turnover)
sand bed for natural grazing behaviour

diet.notes: Uses individually movable bristle-like teeth to sift, sort, and scrape algal films, detritus, and small invertebrates from hard substrate. Marine-based seaweed (nori, ogo) and spirulina-rich herbivore preparations are generally advised at least three times per week. Has been reported to accept algae-based flakes/pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and small meaty items (mysis, brine) alongside tankmates. Cannot survive on live-rock grazing alone — supplemental algae feeding is commonly recommended. Vitamin-supplemented diets are commonly suggested to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion.

goodWith: non-Ctenochaetus tangs (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured surgeonfish

summary: A widely distributed Indo-Pacific bristletooth surgeonfish with an olive-brown body, fine wavy blue lines, and characteristic orange spots near the eyes. Generally considered reef-safe and reasonably tractable for medium-to-large reef systems, but conspecific aggression and HLLE susceptibility apply.
careNotes:
The Orange Stripe Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus striatus) is the most widely distributed member of the bristletooth genus, ranging from the Red Sea and East Africa across the Indo-Pacific to French Polynesia (excluding the Hawaiian, Marquesas, and Easter Islands). FishBase records a maximum length of 26 cm TL with a common length around 18 cm; retail sources commonly state about 10 inches (~25 cm), while Quality Marine reports an aquarium adult size of 7–8 inches (~18–20 cm).

A tank of around 500 L (~130 gal) with a long horizontal footprint is generally advised. Retail sources span 125 gal (Aquatics Unlimited, Quality Marine) to 150 gal (LiveAquaria), with ReefApp recommending 500 L. The cautious-larger choice of 500 L reflects this species' status as one of the larger Ctenochaetus and its needs for open swimming space, strong flow (10–20x turnover commonly recommended), and abundant live rock for natural grazing routes.

Water parameters should be stable and within standard reef ranges (SG ~1.022–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12). Temperature is commonly recommended around 24–27°C; retail sources specify 72–78°F (22–26°C) while FishBase's modelled preferred range is 24.6–29°C with a mean near 27.8°C — averaged to a practical 24–27°C aquarium target.

Diet should lean heavily on marine algae. Dried seaweed (nori, ogo) and spirulina-based foods at least three times per week are generally advised; vitamin-rich diets are commonly suggested to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition this species (like most Acanthurids) is reported to develop in poor water quality or on a vitamin-deficient diet. Quality Marine notes the species does not readily consume hair algae, and may shred it without ingesting it.

The species is generally considered reef-safe and is best kept singly per tank. Conspecific aggression and intolerance of other Ctenochaetus species is high; aggression toward unrelated reef fish is typically modest, particularly when introduced together. Juveniles display shimmering blue-grey bodies with pronounced wide orange body stripes and orange spots around the eyes; adults transition to an olive-brown body with fine wavy blue lines extending head to tail, retaining the orange eye spots.

As an acanthurid, this species possesses sharp caudal-peduncle blades (the 'scalpel') used for defence. They are not venomous but can inflict lacerations on handlers and tankmates during conflicts; handle with care during transfers. The species is also notable as a key link in the ciguatera food chain — relevant in fisheries contexts, not in aquaria.

Cross-source notes: Max size ranged from ~18 cm (Quality Marine aquarium adults) to ~26 cm TL (FishBase wild). Tank minimum spread 125–150 gal across retail (473–568 L); ReefApp 500 L. Per the cautious-larger rule and the active-swimming context, 500 L selected. Temperature spread 22–29°C; averaged to 24–27°C. Care level consistently reported as moderate across LiveAquaria, Aquatics Unlimited, and Quality Marine — recorded as intermediate.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in available hobby sources. FishBase reports the species spawns near the full moon in schools of thousands at variable depths in the wild, with maturity reported around 10+ cm — a pelagic broadcast spawning strategy with planktonic larvae. Spawning in home aquaria is considered highly unlikely given tank-size and group-bonding constraints. Sexes are not visually distinct in the aquarium trade.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Orange Stripe Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus cf. striatus). Provided max size (10 in / ~25 cm), tank minimum (150 gal / 568 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), diet (herbivore, seaweed 3x/week), temperament (semi-aggressive toward other tangs, peaceful otherwise), reef compatibility (yes), and family (Acanthuridae). Reached via the tangs-surgeons collection root after specific URL was located via search (the collection landing page does not directly list every species).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity (Ctenochaetus striatus Quoy & Gaimard, 1825), family (Acanthuridae) and order (Acanthuriformes), max size (26.0 cm TL, common 18.0 cm), depth range (0–60 m, typical 6–30 m), preferred temperature (24.6–29°C, mean 27.8°C), Indo-Pacific native range including the Red Sea (excluding Hawaiian, Marquesas, Easter), habitat (reef flats, lagoons, seaward reefs over coral/rock/pavement/rubble), IUCN status (Least Concern, 2010), and reproduction (lunar spawning in large schools, maturity ~10+ cm).

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Retailer secondary used for tank-size triangulation. Confirmed max size ~10 in, minimum tank 125 gal (~473 L; slightly smaller than LiveAquaria's 150 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), care level (moderate), semi-aggressive, reef-safe.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Wholesale source / care article. Confirmed minimum tank 125 gal with strong flow (10–20x turnover), juvenile vs adult coloration (blue-grey juvenile with orange stripes → olive adult with fine blue lines and orange eye spots), aquarium adult size 7–8 in, peaceful with non-conspecifics when introduced together, intolerant of other Bristletooth Tangs, generally peaceful with corals/clams. Notes the species shreds but does not eat hair algae.

additional.name: Reef App
additional.notes: Hobbyist database secondary used for tank-size and temperament triangulation. Listed tank volume 500 L (~132 gal), max size 26 cm, reef-safe, peaceful-to-mildly-aggressive temperament, herbivorous diet on micro- and macroalgae with supplementary frozen meaty foods, average hardiness.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records found in available hobby sources. Pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild (FishBase reports lunar-phase spawning in large schools); aquarium spawning is considered highly unlikely.
